serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Kebab Rice Set is approximately 400 g, comprising a portion of biryani rice, two pieces of chicken tikka, curry sauce, mint sauce, and a side salad, as typically served at Singapore hawker stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Kebab Rice Set — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Kebab Rice Set (cr_id G00010662). All per-100g values are taken from the HPB canonical record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from ICMR-NIN chicken tikka and biryani rice component data as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite dish; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson, Foster-Powell & Brand-Miller; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati/biryani rice
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kebab Rice Set as a composite dish. GI estimated at 56 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate component: biryani/basmati rice, which has a published GI of approximately 50–58 in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The high fat (26.4 g/100g) and protein (10.7 g/100g) content of the overall set further attenuates glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 56 × 20.96 g / 100 ≈ 11.7 (serving size 400 g, carbohydrate 5.24 g/100g × 4 = 20.96 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg per 100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; a single 400 g serving delivers approximately 4320 mg sodium, nearly double the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg, and requests for reduced sauce or sauce on the side are essential to lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Available carbohydrate is low at 5.24 g/100g, but the biryani rice component carries a medium GI (~56) and portion size determines total glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes may consume this dish in controlled portions (200–300 g) as the high fat and protein content attenuates glycaemic response, but should monitor rice portion size carefully and avoid sugary chutneys or sauces that could elevate postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 26.4 g/100g with estimated saturated fat ~9.5 g/100g and sodium 1080 mg/100g pose combined atherogenic and hypertensive cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to its high saturated fat and very high sodium content; if consumed, portion size should not exceed 200 g and should be paired with a low-sodium, low-fat meal context for the rest of the day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~9.5 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold; high sodium (1080 mg/100g) further promotes tissue inflammation and fluid retention
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish in its standard preparation; requesting grilled rather than fried kebab meat and sauce on the side can reduce saturated fat and sodium load, and substituting lamb with chicken reduces arachidonic acid intake if lamb is used.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kebab Rice Set is a popular Singaporean Indian-Muslim hawker dish consisting of fragrant biryani rice served with two pieces of grilled or tandoor-cooked chicken tikka, a rich curry sauce, cooling mint chutney, and a fresh side salad. The biryani rice is typically cooked with whole spices such as cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and bay leaves, giving it a golden colour and aromatic depth. The chicken tikka is marinated in yogurt and spices before grilling, resulting in tender, slightly charred pieces with a smoky, spiced flavour. The dish is commonly found at Indian-Muslim hawker stalls and food courts across Singapore and is served as a complete meal for lunch or dinner. Nutritionally, the set is energy-dense at 302 kcal per 100 g, driven primarily by its high fat content of 26.4 g/100g, with moderate protein at 10.7 g/100g and notably low available carbohydrate at 5.24 g/100g — reflecting the relatively small rice portion relative to the protein and sauce components. Sodium is very high at 1080 mg/100g, making this dish unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. The dish is halal-certified in most hawker contexts. It is culturally associated with the Indian-Muslim community in Singapore and is a staple of the local multicultural food landscape.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical record for Kebab Rice Set (cr_id G00010662) with a stated confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from ICMR-NIN component data for chicken tikka and biryani rice as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite dish; this single estimated field reduces overall confidence marginally but the primary macros remain high-confidence. GI is an analogous estimate based on the biryani/basmati rice component from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this dish; this is treated as a confirmed zero per the HPB source, likely reflecting the limited fibre contribution of white biryani rice and the small salad component in the measured sample. 2. saturated_fat_g (9.5 g/100g) is estimated from ICMR-NIN data for chicken tikka (~8–11 g/100g saturated fat range) and biryani rice components, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite dish. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite hawker dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or ICMR-NIN as of 2026. 4. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite hawker dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or available in ICMR-NIN for this exact preparation as of 2026. 5. GI of 56 is an analogous estimate based on basmati/biryani rice GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008); the high fat and protein content of the full set is expected to further attenuate the glycaemic response in practice. 6. The dish is typically halal in Singapore hawker contexts but halal certification should be verified at point of purchase.
